primary_diagnosis.name: Acute autonomic and visceral flare of hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (dysautonomia with gastroparesis, orthostatic intolerance and vomiting-related dehydration)

comorbidities.name: Dehydration / volume depletion secondary to intractable vomiting
Functional bladder dysfunction / incomplete emptying (pelvic floor / autonomic)

pathophysiology_summary: Hypermobile Ehlers-Danlos syndrome (hEDS) is a heritable connective-tissue disorder in which abnormal collagen affects not only joints and skin but also autonomic and visceral function. This patient is in an acute flare of the well-recognised hEDS dysautonomia/visceral phenotype: impaired gastric and intestinal motility (gastroparesis) produces intractable nausea, bloating, a distended tympanic abdomen and repeated non-bilious vomiting, while autonomic dysfunction produces orthostatic intolerance (marked postural tachycardia and orthostatic hypotension: supine 100/60 with HR 110, standing 85/50 with HR 135) and presyncope. Pelvic-floor/autonomic dysfunction gives incomplete bladder emptying. The acute danger is NOT the chronic disorder itself but its consequence: ~12 hours of inability to retain any oral fluid has produced clinically significant volume depletion (dry mucous membranes, reduced urine output, orthostatic drop), which — untreated and un-rehydrated — trends toward hypovolaemic shock and electrolyte derangement. Crucially, the acute-abdomen screen is negative: no faeculent/bilious vomiting, flatus passed, and no localised peritonism, rebound or guarding — arguing strongly against mechanical bowel obstruction. The correct clinical task is to attribute the flare to the known chronic condition, exclude a surgical abdomen on the negative red-flag screen, and treat the genuine acute problem (dehydration + intractable vomiting) with parenteral antiemesis and rehydration — none of which telehealth can deliver, so the honest disposition is urgent same-day IN-PERSON care.

differential.diagnosis: Acute flare of known hEDS dysautonomia/visceral phenotype (gastroparesis + orthostatic intolerance) with dehydration
differential.evidence_basis: Known connective-tissue disorder with recurrent gut + orthostatic flares; current picture of intractable vomiting, bloating and postural dizziness fits the established phenotype. Leading, but must not be closed on until a surgical abdomen and dehydration severity are assessed.

Vomiting + distended tympanic abdomen + cramping pain could represent obstruction — a surgical emergency that must be actively excluded, especially as telehealth cannot examine the abdomen or image it.

differential.diagnosis: Hypovolaemia / volume depletion from ongoing vomiting
differential.evidence_basis: Inability to keep fluids down for ~12 h with reduced urine output and orthostatic symptoms is the genuine acute threat and drives disposition regardless of the underlying cause.
Common cause of vomiting — but no fever and no diarrhoea, and a clear background of chronic fluctuating dysmotility make it less likely.

Vomiting with tachycardia warrants excluding a metabolic emergency; source note documented a normal capillary glucose (5.5 mmol/L) and no Kussmaul breathing on in-person assessment, lowering this — but telehealth cannot check glucose or ketones.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The AI Doctor should hold the known-hEDS flare as leading WHILE keeping bowel obstruction, hypovolaemia and a metabolic emergency explicitly on the list. Prematurely closing on 'just her usual flare, manage at home' (missing dehydration) OR jumping to 'surgical emergency, call 000' (over-triage) are both errors at this stage.

differential.diagnosis: Acute hEDS dysautonomia/visceral flare (gastroparesis) with clinically significant dehydration
differential.evidence_basis: Non-bilious non-bloody vomit, flatus passed, cramping generalised (not localised) pain and a known recurrent phenotype point to a functional dysmotility flare; the dominant actionable problem is the dehydration from failed oral intake plus orthostatic compromise.

Made less likely by non-faeculent vomit, passage of flatus and absence of localised peritonism — but it CANNOT be fully excluded remotely, which is one reason in-person assessment is required rather than home management.
No fever, no diarrhoea, and a clear chronic-dysmotility background make infective gastroenteritis unlikely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After screening, the flare should be leading, but bowel obstruction stays as an important_not_to_miss precisely because telehealth cannot examine or image the abdomen — this is a legitimate driver toward urgent in-person assessment (T3), NOT a reason to call 000 in the absence of any positive surgical red flag.

differential.diagnosis: Acute hEDS dysautonomia/visceral flare with dehydration requiring parenteral antiemesis and rehydration
differential.evidence_basis: Working formulation for telehealth: recognise the flare, exclude a surgical abdomen on the negative screen, and act on the dehydration — which needs in-person parenteral treatment the AI Doctor cannot provide. Definitive confirmation (bloods/electrolytes, and imaging only if the surgical question persists) occurs in person.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct final formulation is a known-condition flare complicated by dehydration, dispositioned to urgent same-day IN-PERSON care. A plan that ends in home self-care with sips of fluid (ignoring 12 h of failed oral intake) is under-triage; a plan that ends in a 000 ambulance for a presumed surgical abdomen with a negative screen is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Unable to tolerate ANY oral fluids for ~12 hours with reduced urine output — established, ongoing volume depletion
red_flags.clinical_significance: This is the decisive acute finding: telehealth cannot rehydrate a patient who cannot keep fluids down. Ongoing failure of oral intake plus oliguria means the patient needs parenteral rehydration and antiemesis — an in-person, same-day intervention — and cannot be safely left on home sips-of-fluid advice.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: AI attributes it to 'the usual flare', advises small sips at home and GP review in a couple of days. The patient continues to vomit, cannot rehydrate, and progresses to hypovolaemic shock, syncopal collapse (fall risk, lives alone) and electrolyte derangement.

red_flags.description: Marked orthostatic symptoms — heart racing/pounding and near-blackout on standing, relieved by lying flat
red_flags.clinical_significance: Reported postural tachycardia/presyncope is the patient-obtainable proxy for the clinician-measured orthostatic drop (supine 100/60 HR110 → standing 85/50 HR135). It signals haemodynamic effect of volume depletion superimposed on baseline dysautonomia and, combined with living alone, is a significant fall/collapse risk.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Orthostatic compromise under-weighted; patient told to 'take it easy at home', stands to reach the bathroom, syncopes and is injured with no one present to help.

red_flags.description: Bladder feels full but cannot be emptied (incomplete voiding / possible retention)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Incomplete bladder emptying in this context is largely functional (pelvic-floor/autonomic) and compounded by dehydration, but genuine urinary retention needs in-person assessment (bladder scan / catheterisation) that telehealth cannot perform — another driver toward in-person review.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Retention overlooked; ongoing discomfort and, if truly obstructive, a distended bladder left undrained.

red_flags.description: SURGICAL-ABDOMEN escalation triggers — bilious (green) or bloody vomiting, or pain becoming sharp and localised
red_flags.clinical_significance: These are the features that WOULD convert this to a surgical/obstruction emergency (T4/T5). They are currently ABSENT (non-bilious, non-bloody vomit; generalised cramping, not localised; flatus passed) — which is exactly why the correct baseline is urgent in-person care, not a 000 ambulance. Their appearance is the mandatory escalation trigger.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: If these develop later and were not safety-netted, an evolving obstruction or GI bleed could be missed.

red_flags.description: Signs of established shock or decompensation — actual syncope/collapse, confusion, cold clammy shutdown, or no urine at all
red_flags.clinical_significance: The patient is compromised but compensated supine (supine BP maintained). Frank shock, syncope or confusion would mandate immediate T5 (000). Their absence supports T3 with tight safety-netting rather than an emergency ambulance now.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to safety-net these leaves a deteriorating patient without a clear 000 trigger.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Accepting 'known hEDS flare, she gets these' and defaulting to home self-care (sips of fluid, GP in a few days) — ignoring that she has failed ALL oral intake for 12 h with orthostatic compromise and reduced urine output, which telehealth cannot treat.

Being driven by 'severe abdominal pain + intractable vomiting + tachycardia + a rule-out-obstruction framing' to a reflexive 000 ambulance for a surgical abdomen, when the surgical red-flag screen is negative (non-bilious/non-bloody vomit, flatus passed, no localised peritonism).

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Prescribing an oral antiemetic as the fix
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Recommending an oral antiemetic (or telling the patient to just keep sipping) when she cannot keep anything down — oral therapy will not be absorbed; the definitive treatment is PARENTERAL and therefore in-person.

Attempting to fully manage a patient who needs parenteral rehydration/antiemesis and a hands-on abdominal and bladder assessment that the AI Doctor cannot perform — the correct move is to arrange urgent same-day in-person care, not to substitute remote advice for it.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Opioid or antispasmodic for the pain
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reaching for an opioid or anticholinergic antispasmodic to settle the cramping — both further slow gut motility and worsen gastroparesis, nausea and urinary retention.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Supine BP 100/60 mmHg, HR 110 bpm; standing BP 85/50 mmHg, HR 135 bpm — a >30 bpm postural HR rise and a >15 mmHg systolic drop, consistent with orthostatic intolerance amplified by volume depletion.
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Confirms haemodynamically significant orthostatic compromise in person. NOT obtainable via telehealth — the AI Doctor must infer circulatory effect from the reported near-blackout on standing, not from a measured value, and must not claim a measured BP.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Orthostatic blood pressure and heart rate (supine vs standing) — the source in-person values were 100/60 HR110 supine, 85/50 HR135 standing
Abdominal examination — distension, tympany, bowel sounds, and the presence/absence of peritonism, rebound or guarding needed to exclude a surgical abdomen
Palpable bladder / degree of urinary retention (bladder scan)
Capillary blood glucose and ketones to exclude a metabolic emergency (source measured glucose 5.5 mmol/L in person)
Serum electrolytes and renal function to quantify dehydration and derangement
Ability to administer IM/IV antiemetics or IV rehydration
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange URGENT SAME-DAY IN-PERSON assessment (T3 — urgent care / GP with facilities / ED depending on access) for parenteral antiemesis and rehydration and a hands-on abdominal and bladder assessment; escalate to ED/000 (T4/T5) if any surgical red flag, syncope, or signs of shock develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The main risk is NOT diagnostic (the flare is recognisable) but DISPOSITION in both directions: under-triage to home self-care despite failed oral intake, or over-triage to a 000 surgical-abdomen call despite a negative surgical screen.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal — nausea, vomiting and gastroparesis 2024
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Intractable vomiting with inability to maintain oral intake and signs of dehydration warrants parenteral antiemesis and rehydration and in-person assessment; oral antiemetics are inappropriate when nothing is retained.

guidelines_referenced.reference: RACGP: Ehlers-Danlos syndromes and hypermobility — GP management resources
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: hEDS is managed longitudinally with multidisciplinary/specialist input; acute flares are attributed to the known condition after excluding alternative acute pathology, with escalation reserved for genuine decompensation.
